posts.title: ខេត្តកំពត៖ ទីក្រុងស្រស់ស្អាតនៃប្រទេសកម្ពុជា
posts.content: ខេត្តកំពត គឺជាខេត្តមួយស្ថិតនៅប៉ែកខាងត្បូងនៃប្រទេសកម្ពុជា ដែលមានចម្ងាយ ១៤៨ គ.ម. ពីទីក្រុងភ្នំពេញ មានព្រំប្រទល់ខាងជើងទល់នឹងស្រុកបរសេដ្ឋ ខេត្តកំពង់ស្ពឺ ខាងកើតជាប់នឹងស្រុកត្រាំកក់ និង ស្រុកគីរីវង់ ខេត្តតាកែវ ខាងត្បូងជាប់នឹងឈូងសមុទ្រ ខាងលិចជាប់នឹងស្រុកកំពង់សិលា និងស្រុកព្រៃនប់ ខេត្តព្រះសីហនុ ដូចគ្នាដែរ ខ្សែឆ្នេរដ៏វែងស្ថិតនៅលើឈូងសមុទ្រថៃទៅខាងត្បូង ខេត្តកំពតមានទីក្រុងឈ្មោះក្រុងកំពត។ កំពតមានប្រជាជន ៦២៧ ៨៨៤ នាក់[២] និងប្រកបដោយស្រុកនិងក្រុងចំនួនប្រាំបីដែលបានចែកទៅជាឃុំ-សង្កាត់ ៩២ ជាមួយនិងភូមិសរុប ៤៧៧ ភូមិ។[៣] ខេត្ត​កំពត​មាន​សំណង់​ប្រវត្តិសាស្រ្តជាច្រើន​ដូចជា ​ផ្ទះ ផ្សារ​លក់​ទំនិញ រោងភាពយន្ត​ សាលារៀន​ ផ្លូវគមនាគមន៏ មន្ទីរពេទ្យជាដើម​ សុទ្ធ​សឹង​​ជា​ភស្ដុតាង]បង្ហាញ​ឱ្យ​ឃើញ​ពី​ការ​ចាប់​អារម្មណ៍​របស់​ពួក​បារាំង​ទៅ​លើ​ទឹក​ដី​ខ្មែរខេត្ត​កំពតមួយ​នេះ​។​ហើយក៏មានភ្នំបូកគោដែលជាទីចាប់អារម្មណ៍របស់ភ្ញៀវទេសចរណ៍ជាតិ និងអន្តរជាតិគ្រប់ៗរូបផងដែរ។ នៅលើនោះក៏មានកាស៊ីណូ និង មាន កន្លែងកម្សាន្តជាច្រើនដូចជា៖ វាលស្រែមួយរយ ទឹកធ្លាក់អាចមើលទេសភាព មកខាងក្រោមយ៉ាងស្រស់ស្អាត អាចចាប់ពពកបាន។-ល-។ ស្រុកទូកមាសបានតាំងទីនៅខេត្តកំពត។ទេសភាពនៃព្រែកកំពត មើលពីក្រុងកំពត ក្បែរសាលាខេត្ត ចំពីមុខភ្នំបូកគោ។ ខេត្ដតាកែវ បានចូលដែនរដ្ឋបាលខេត្ដកំពត នៅត្រង់ស្រុកជុំគី ត្រង់ចំនុចវត្ដពុទ្ធគីរី រហូតដល់ រមណីយដ្ឋានកំពូលពេជ្រ។
posts.author: Alex Morgan

posts.title: ស្ពានរថភ្លើង ខេត្តកំពត
posts.content:
ស្ពានរថភ្លើង ខេត្តកំពតគឺជាស្ពានមួយដែលមានវ័យចំណាស់មិនចាញ់ស្ពានឥន្ធនូឡើយ ហើយទីនោះមានទេសភាពអំណោយផលល្អមែនទែន ជាពិសេសនាពេលល្ងាចថ្ងៃរៀបលិច។

ស្ពាននោះមិនត្រឹមតែជាស្ពានសម្រាប់អោយរថភ្លើងឆ្លងកាត់ប៉ុណ្ណោះទេ វាថែមទាំងជាកន្លែងដែលមានភាពពេញនិយមសម្រាប់អ្នកដែលចូលចិត្តថតរូប អង្គុយលេង និងស្រូបខ្យល់អាកាសថែមទៀតផង។

គេសង្កេតឃើញថាភ្ញៀវទេសចរដែលបានធ្វើដំណើរកំសាន្តទៅតំបន់ទឹកឈូ ហើយត្រលប់មកវិញតែងតែឈៀងចូលលេង ឬទស្សនានៅទីនោះ។ ក្រៅពីនេះក៏មានបងប្អូនយើងមួយចំនួនគាត់បានជ្រើសរើសយកទីកន្លែងនោះជាកន្លែងថតរូបសម្រាប់អាពាហ៍ពិពាហ៍របស់ពួកគាត់ក្នុងចំណោមកន្លែងស្អាតៗដ៏ទៃទៀតផងដែរ។ គួរបញ្ជាក់ផងដែរថាស្ពានដ៏វ័យចំណាស់មួយនេះក៏ធ្លាប់បានបាក់ម្តងរួមមកហើយដែរកាលពីឆ្នាំ១៩៩៨។
posts.author: Alex Morgan

posts.title: The Invisible Architecture of Good Design
posts.content:
The best design is the kind you never notice. Not because it's boring, but because it fits so perfectly into your expectations that your brain stops questioning it entirely. A well-designed door doesn't make you wonder whether to push or pull. A well-designed app doesn't make you hunt for the back button. These aren't accidents — they're the result of thousands of small decisions, each one invisible on its own, that collectively create an experience that feels effortless.

I've been thinking about this a lot lately because I've been redesigning my portfolio, and the temptation to add — more animations, more gradients, more visual interest — is almost overwhelming. Every design tool puts a universe of possibilities at your fingertips, and the instinct is to use all of them. But restraint is where the real craft lives.

Dieter Rams understood this. His ten principles for good design still hold up because they're not about aesthetics — they're about respect for the person using the thing. 'Good design is as little design as possible' isn't a call for minimalism as a style; it's a recognition that every unnecessary element is a small tax on someone's attention, and attention is the most finite resource we have.

The next time you use something that just works — a pen that glides, a website that loads before you notice, a room that makes you exhale — take a moment to appreciate the invisible architecture behind it. Someone spent hours making sure you'd never have to think about it. That's not nothing. That's everything.
posts.author: Alex Morgan

posts.title: Letters from Kyoto: Finding Silence in the City
posts.content:
Kyoto has a kind of silence that I've never encountered anywhere else. It's not the absence of sound — there are trains and bicycles and school children and the occasional tourist exclaiming over something — but rather a quality of attention that the city seems to cultivate in its visitors. You arrive loud and restless, and within a day, without quite knowing how, your internal volume dial has turned down by several notches.

I spent most of my first morning at Ryoan-ji, the famous rock garden. Fifteen rocks arranged on raked white gravel, and you can never see all of them from any single position. I sat on the wooden veranda for over an hour, watching other visitors do the same thing — sit, look, leave. Nobody talked. The garden doesn't invite interpretation. It just asks you to be present, and somehow that's enough.

Later that week, I wandered into a small tea shop in Higashiyama and was served matcha by a woman who moved with such deliberate precision that making tea looked like a form of meditation. Each gesture was practiced and unhurried. The bowl was warm in my hands, the foam was perfect, and for a few minutes, the entire world contracted to the size of that small ceramic circle.

I came to Kyoto looking for temples and gardens. I left understanding that silence isn't something you find in a place — it's something a place can remind you how to find in yourself. The city doesn't give you peace. It shows you where you left it.
posts.author: Alex Morgan

posts.title: On Writing, Vulnerability, and the Stories We Owe Ourselves
posts.content:
Every writer I know struggles with the same question, though they express it differently: How honest am I willing to be? Not honest in the factual sense — though that matters — but honest in the emotional sense. Am I willing to write the thing I'm actually feeling, or am I going to write the version that makes me look better, smarter, more composed?

I've deleted more first drafts than I've published. Not because the writing was bad, but because it was safe. It was the literary equivalent of a LinkedIn post — professionally optimistic, carefully curated, stripped of the mess and contradiction that make a human life actually worth reading about. The best writing I've ever done has always started with a feeling I didn't want to admit to.

There's a misconception that vulnerability in writing means oversharing or being confessional for its own sake. But real vulnerability is more specific than that. It's not about telling everyone your secrets; it's about being precise about the truth of an experience, even when that precision makes you uncomfortable. It's the difference between saying 'I was sad' and describing the exact quality of a Tuesday afternoon when you realized something you'd been hoping for wasn't going to happen.

We owe it to ourselves to tell our stories honestly, not because the world needs them (though it might), but because the act of telling them is how we understand who we are. Every story you soften, every detail you sand down, every emotion you rewrite to be more palatable — that's a small theft from your own life. Write the real thing. You can always delete it later.
posts.author: Alex Morgan

posts.title: A Kitchen Philosophy: What Cooking Taught Me About Patience
posts.content:
I burned my first onion. Not just slightly overcooked — I turned it into a small blackened mass that set off the smoke alarm and prompted my roommate to ask, with genuine concern, whether I was trying to burn the building down. I was twenty-three years old and had never cooked a meal that didn't involve a microwave.

That was ten years ago. Since then, cooking has become the most important practice in my life — more than meditation, more than journaling, more than any self-improvement book I've ever read. Not because I became a great cook (I'm decent, at best), but because cooking taught me something no book could: patience is not waiting. Patience is paying attention to something that takes time.

A proper braise takes three hours. You can't rush it. You can turn up the heat and try, but all you'll get is tough meat and shallow flavor. The collagen needs time to break down. The flavors need time to meld. The liquid needs time to reduce into something that coats the back of a spoon. Every step requires your presence but not your interference. You watch, you adjust, you wait. And then, eventually, something transforms.

I think about this whenever I'm frustrated with how slowly things are moving in my life — career, relationships, creative projects. We live in a culture that treats waiting as failure, as if the only valid state is forward motion. But some of the most important transformations in life are like a braise. They need heat and time and attention, and if you try to force them, you just end up with something tough and shallow. Trust the process. Stir occasionally. And for god's sake, don't turn up the heat.
posts.author: Alex Morgan

posts.title: The Case for Reading Books You Don't Understand
posts.content:
There's a particular kind of frustration that comes from reading a book that's clearly brilliant but equally clearly over your head. You turn the pages, nodding at sentences you only half-comprehend, feeling both illuminated and inadequate in equal measure. Most people put the book down at this point and return to something more comfortable. I'm here to argue that you should keep going.

I first experienced this with Virginia Woolf's 'The Waves.' I was nineteen, and the book felt like trying to hold water. The sentences were gorgeous — achingly, almost unbearably beautiful — but the structure dissolved the moment I tried to grasp it. I read the whole thing in a state of bewildered admiration, understanding maybe sixty percent of what was happening on any given page.

But here's the thing: that sixty percent changed me. The way Woolf wrote about consciousness — the fluidity of it, the way thought bleeds into sensation and memory and perception without clear boundaries — it rewired something in how I experienced my own inner life. I started noticing things I hadn't noticed before. The way a memory can arrive fully formed, triggered by nothing. The way two people in the same room are having entirely different experiences of the same moment.

A book you fully understand confirms what you already know. A book you partially understand expands the perimeter of what's possible. Read above your level. Not as a chore or a performance, but as an act of faith that understanding comes in layers, and the first layer — the one where you're simply in the presence of something too large to take in all at once — is its own kind of reward.
posts.author: Alex Morgan
